commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds an argv-sensitive exact `agents add` startup policy that skips plugin preload for explicit config-only add flags and adds focused startup/pre-action regression tests.

PR surface: Source +25, Tests +182. Total +207 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main preloads plugins for `agents add`, and the PR body supplies terminal proof for the no-bind config-only case; I did not rerun commands because this review was required to keep the checkout read-only.
commands.review_followup: - There is a narrow, automatable blocker: preserve existing plugin-aware `--bind` account resolution while keeping the no-bind config-only preload skip.

target.title: fix(cli): skip plugin preload for config-only agents add
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #66936.

- Add an exact startup policy for `agents add` that skips plugin preload only when argv selects the documented config-only/non-interactive add path.
- Keep interactive `agents add` on the existing all-plugin preload path so the wizard can still see plugin-backed setup/auth surfaces.
- Add focused startup-policy and pre-action regression tests covering separated/equal flag forms, `--json` alone, and flags after `--`.

## AI Assistance Disclosure

Prepared with AI assistance. I reviewed and understand the affected path from CLI pre-action startup policy through `agents add` command registration and the config-only add command body.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw agents add <id> --workspace <dir> --non-interactive --json` no longer preloads plugins before the config-only flow runs.

Real environment tested: Built CLI via `node dist/entry.js` on Linux with isolated `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`; no real credentials or user OpenClaw state were used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
pnpm build

timeout 4s env \
  HOME="$HOME_DIR" \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$STATE_DIR" \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$CONFIG_PATH" \
  HOLD_OPEN_MARKER="$CONTROL_MARKER" \
  OPENCLAW_HIDE_BANNER=1 \
  node dist/entry.js agents add interactive-smoke

timeout 8s env \
  HOME="$HOME_DIR" \
  OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$STATE_DIR" \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$CONFIG_PATH" \
  HOLD_OPEN_MARKER="$FIX_MARKER" \
  OPENCLAW_HIDE_BANNER=1 \
  node dist/entry.js agents add smoke --workspace "$WORKSPACE_DIR" --non-interactive --json
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
control_status=124
control_marker_exists=yes
fixed_status=0
fixed_marker_exists=no
fixed_stdout_first_lines=
{
  "agentId": "smoke",
  "name": "smoke",
  "workspace": "$RUN_DIR/workspaces/smoke",
  "agentDir": "$RUN_DIR/state/agents/smoke/agent",
  "bindings": {
    "added": [],
    "updated": [],
    "skipped": [],
    "conflicts": []
  }
}
proof_passed=1
```

Observed result after fix: The control command proves plugin preload is observable and can hold the process open. The config-only `agents add` command exits 0, writes the expected JSON payload, and does not import the proof plugin.

What was not tested: I attempted the remote `blacksmith-testbox` changed gate through Crabbox, but this machine had no authenticated Blacksmith CLI. The CLI installed successfully, then `blacksmith auth login --non-interactive --organization openclaw` timed out waiting for browser/API-token auth. GitHub PR checks should cover the remote changed gate after the draft PR is opened.

## Root Cause

`registerPreActionHooks` resolves startup policy before the `agents add` action computes whether the command is interactive or config-only. The command catalog had a broad parent `agents` policy with `loadPlugins: "always"`, and no exact `agents add` override. As a result, config-only invocations inherited plugin preload before reaching `agentsAddCommand`, so a plugin import/auth problem could hang a command that only needed to update config.

## Dependency contract

No external dependency behavior is needed for this fix. The relevant source contracts are internal:

- `src/cli/program/register.agent.ts` selects the non-interactive/config-only `agents add` path when explicit add flags are present: `workspace`, `model`, `agentDir`, `bind`, or `nonInteractive`.
- `src/cli/argv.ts` helpers support both `--flag value` and `--flag=value`, and stop scanning at `--`.
- `src/cli/command-bootstrap.ts` returns before plugin registry loading when startup policy resolves `loadPlugins: false`.

## Regression Test Plan

- `src/cli/command-path-policy.test.ts`: verifies the exact `agents add` policy merge and preserves the parent network-proxy bypass.
- `src/cli/command-startup-policy.test.ts`: verifies interactive add and `--json` alone still load plugins, config-only add flags skip plugins, and flags after `--` are ignored.
- `src/cli/command-execution-startup.test.ts`: verifies startup context distinguishes interactive and config-only add.
- `src/cli/program/preaction.test.ts`: verifies pre-action skips plugin registry loading for config-only add but still loads plugins for interactive add.

## Security Impact

This reduces accidental plugin code execution for a config-only CLI path. It does not change plugin trust policy, plugin registry behavior, credentials, SecretRef resolution, or provider auth handling.

## Human Verification

I checked the issue context, exact open/closed PR searches for `66936`, prior merged PR #74220, and the current command source before patching. No open PR currently addresses this residual `agents add` startup path.

## CODEOWNERS / owner review

No direct `.github/CODEOWNERS` rule matches the touched `src/cli/**` files. Normal CLI/startup-policy maintainer review is expected.

## Changelog

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit. This is contributor PR release-note material only.

## Review conversations

No GitHub review conversations exist for this PR yet. Local review checks:

- `autoreview`: clean, no accepted/actionable findings.
- `codex review --base origin/main`: no actionable correctness issues found.

## Risks

- The startup policy now intentionally mirrors the explicit config-only flag list from `register.agent.ts`; future add flags that select the config-only path must update both places.
- The fix is argv-sensitive by design, so tests cover `--flag value`, `--flag=value`, and `--` terminator behavior.

## Behavior tradeoffs

- `--json` alone is not treated as config-only and still follows the interactive add preload path.
- Interactive `agents add` still preloads plugins so plugin-backed setup/auth surfaces remain available to the wizard.

## Scope boundaries

- Does not change `agents list`, SecretRef behavior, Telegram status paths, provider status rendering, config schema, or plugin loader internals.
- Does not add compatibility shims or broad plugin lifecycle changes.

## Validation

```sh
pnpm docs:list
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/command-startup-policy.test.ts src/cli/command-path-policy.test.ts src/cli/command-execution-startup.test.ts src/cli/program/preaction.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cli/command-catalog.ts src/cli/command-path-policy.test.ts src/cli/command-startup-policy.test.ts src/cli/command-execution-startup.test.ts src/cli/program/preaction.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm build
# real CLI proof shown above
```

Results: focused Vitest passed 4 files / 49 tests, format check passed, `git diff --check` passed, build passed, and the isolated real CLI proof passed.
target.author: ferminquant

commands.author: LaPhilosophie

target.title: fix(gateway): require admin scope for browser proxy invoke
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `browser.request` is registered as an `operator.admin` gateway method, but the same `browser.proxy` node-host command was also reachable through direct `node.invoke`, whose core method scope is `operator.write`.
- Solution: require `operator.admin` before forwarding direct `node.invoke` requests for `browser.proxy`.
- What changed: direct `node.invoke` calls to `browser.proxy` now fail before node dispatch unless the caller has `operator.admin`; existing persistent browser profile mutation blocking remains unchanged.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): `browser.request` behavior is unchanged; node-host browser proxy implementation is unchanged; admin-scoped callers can still invoke `browser.proxy` through the normal node command path.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The browser gateway surface intentionally treats `browser.request` as admin-scoped. That method can route to a node-hosted browser through the `browser.proxy` node command when a browser-capable node is connected.

Before this patch, a scoped operator token with only `operator.write` could not call `browser.request`, but could call the same underlying `browser.proxy` command through direct `node.invoke`. That creates an authorization mismatch between two routes to the same browser proxy capability.

This PR keeps the route semantics aligned: if the browser gateway method requires `operator.admin`, the direct node invoke route for the same browser proxy capability also requires `operator.admin`.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: direct `node.invoke` to `browser.proxy` now follows the same admin scope boundary as `browser.request`.
- Real environment tested: local macOS development checkout, branch `browser-proxy-admin-scope`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server.node-invoke-approval-bypass.test.ts --reporter verbose`
  - `npm exec --yes pnpm@11.1.0 -- oxfmt --check src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.ts src/gateway/server.node-invoke-approval-bypass.test.ts`
  - `npm exec --yes pnpm@11.1.0 -- node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`
- Evidence after fix:

```text
✓ |gateway| src/gateway/server.node-invoke-approval-bypass.test.ts > node.invoke approval bypass > rejects browser.proxy persistent profile mutations before forwarding
✓ |gateway| src/gateway/server.node-invoke-approval-bypass.test.ts > node.invoke approval bypass > requires admin scope for direct browser.proxy node.invoke
✓ |gateway| src/gateway/server.node-invoke-approval-bypass.test.ts > node.invoke approval bypass > allows direct browser.proxy node.invoke for admin-scoped operators

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  7 passed (7)
```

Before evidence:

```text
× |gateway| src/gateway/server.node-invoke-approval-bypass.test.ts > node.invoke approval bypass > requires admin scope for direct browser.proxy node.invoke
  → expected true to be false

The write-scoped caller was blocked by browser.request, but direct node.invoke to browser.proxy succeeded and reached the node.
```

- Observed result after fix:
  - A scoped device token with only `operator.write` cannot call `browser.request`.
  - The same write-only token also cannot directly invoke `browser.proxy` through `node.invoke`.
  - The denied direct `browser.proxy` request is rejected before node dispatch.
  - An `operator.admin` scoped token can still invoke `browser.proxy` through direct `node.invoke`.
- What was not tested: a remote multi-device browser deployment. The regression test uses a real local Gateway, scoped device tokens, and a connected node client that declares `browser.proxy`.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: direct `node.invoke` enforced the generic `operator.write` scope for node commands, but did not apply the stricter `operator.admin` scope used by the equivalent `browser.request` gateway route.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test comparing the authorization semantics of `browser.request` and direct `node.invoke` for the same `browser.proxy` backend command.
- Contributing context: existing code already blocked persistent browser profile mutations through direct `node.invoke`; the remaining non-persistent browser proxy paths still needed the route-level admin scope alignment.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server.node-invoke-approval-bypass.test.ts`
- Scenario the test locks in: write-scoped device tokens cannot bypass the admin-scoped `browser.request` route by directly invoking `browser.proxy`; admin-scoped tokens still work.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the mismatch is in Gateway node invocation authorization, so a Gateway test with scoped device tokens and a connected node covers the policy boundary directly.
- Existing test that already covers this: none before this PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Direct `node.invoke` callers now need `operator.admin` to invoke `browser.proxy`. Admin-scoped callers and `browser.request` behavior are unchanged.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
operator.write -> browser.request -> blocked: operator.admin required
operator.write -> node.invoke(browser.proxy) -> node dispatch

After:
operator.write -> browser.request -> blocked: operator.admin required
operator.write -> node.invoke(browser.proxy) -> blocked: operator.admin required
operator.admin -> node.invoke(browser.proxy) -> node dispatch
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes - this narrows direct browser proxy invocation to the same admin scope used by the browser gateway route.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this reduces the callable surface for scoped write-only operator tokens by preventing direct access to the node-hosted browser proxy route.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local development checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: Browser extension / Gateway node invoke
- Relevant config: local test Gateway with a connected node declaring `browser.proxy`

### Steps

1. Start the Gateway test server.
2. Connect a node client declaring `browser.proxy`.
3. Connect an operator device token scoped only to `operator.write`.
4. Verify `browser.request` is rejected with `missing scope: operator.admin`.
5. Verify direct `node.invoke` for `browser.proxy` is also rejected with `missing scope: operator.admin` and is not forwarded to the node.
6. Connect an operator device token scoped to `operator.admin`.
7. Verify direct `node.invoke` for `browser.proxy` still forwards to the node.

### Expected

- Write-only operator callers cannot reach `browser.proxy` through either browser route.
- Admin operator callers can still reach `browser.proxy`.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the targeted Gateway regression test.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: write-only `browser.request` rejection; write-only direct `browser.proxy` rejection before node dispatch; admin direct `browser.proxy` dispatch; persistent browser profile mutation rejection remains unchanged.
- Edge cases checked: connected node declares `browser.proxy`; command is allowlisted so the rejection is specifically the scope check, not command availability.
- What was not verified: remote multi-device browser deployment.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Mostly
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A
- Notes: callers that directly invoke `browser.proxy` through `node.invoke` now need `operator.admin`, matching the existing `browser.request` scope.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a non-admin integration relying on direct `node.invoke` for `browser.proxy` will now be rejected.
  - Mitigation: use an admin-scoped operator token for browser proxy control, or call the existing admin-scoped `browser.request` route.
target.author: laphilosophie
